## [00:00 - 03:01]

So a quick reminder about abandoned cart. If someone's already on your list, they're viewing a product and add something to their cart but they don't start checkout. This will then trigger an abandoned cart series. And this is the second of the two that I mentioned, Clavio or other platforms don't make super easy to add. So again, this is the abandoned cart and abandoned carts work really well because they remind customers of what they were pretty close to actually purchasing. So this helps them kind of get over that ledge and make that purchase. So the first example I have is from a client of ours that sells watches. And one thing that I mentioned before is the trigger. So the trigger in this case is what's called added to cart. And then I also mentioned what's called trigger and filter flows. So for them in particular, you can kind of see right here on the left hand panel, they're basically just excluding people that have a certain kind of watch as well as kind of their closeout sales. So in this flow, we will provide discounts. So they don't want to discount either their really popular products or products that are already on sale. And then the other three filters that you should be mindful of is that someone started checkout zero times. So again, just like what I mentioned with the browse abandonment, we want to prioritize other email flows that are further down the funnel. So if the browse is kind of, hey, I'm interested, but I'm just casually looking, the abandoned cart says, hey, I have interest more than I do for the browse. But the abandoned checkout, which we'll talk about next, has the most intent. And if we can prioritize any of them, we'll prioritize that one. So in this case, that's why I said checkout started zero times since starting this flow. And then the last two is around someone's place in order zero times since starting this flow. Again, if someone's purchased after the first email, we don't want to bog them down with the other emails in this flow since the whole goal of this flow is to drive the conversion. And then lastly, that someone has not already received these same emails in the past two weeks, depending on how aggressive you want to be or not want to be. Anywhere from about two to four weeks makes sense. So that way, if you don't have that, people could technically receive these emails every single time they abandon a cart, which would get pretty annoying. So in this case, you can see that the first email is being triggered one hour after someone abandons their cart. And you can see that this email is done really well. Has a 44% open rate, 11% click through, and a 2.4% placed order rate with a total revenue of $3,200 for the specific email. And again, this is all for the past 30 days. So we'll dive into this email. Again, as you can see, when the two emails are kind of stacked, that means there's an A-B test going on. There's two different versions. In this case, again, there's a subject line test. So one thing I wanted to point out again about the different elements of email is you have the from name. So it typically should be your company name or someone that's client facing or customer facing. So someone's name at the company, so that way they know who it's from. You have the subject line itself and then the pre-hitter text. And those work hand in hand to make sure that people actually open the email. Again, the from name and those two things work together to get people to open. So you don't want to mislead people and be too clickbaity, but you also don't want to be too boring. You want to make sure it's kind of a happy medium to get people to open the email. But again, the subject line and pre-hitter text have to relate to the content of the email or else people are going to be disappointed and they're either going to unsubscribe or not take the action required. And then within the email, specifically for abandoned cart, you should kind of have a clever title or some kind of title. Did you mean to leave this behind? Or as an example in the latest one, it says it's your Wi-Fi okay. You should have text. Typically we see discounts being provided. Again, it could be a dollar off discount. It could be a percentage off discount or it could be a free shipping offer. Make sure to show the product itself. Make sure to have more information about the product as well as the price. And then some kind of call to action to get people to click through and hopefully purchase your product. One other thing that's kind of cool and worth mentioning is test with trying to dynamically render the product name in the call to action button. And just make sure that the button itself isn't too long. So for example, if you want to say someone's the product name, but it's really long, in that case I probably wouldn't.
